text: I typically do a trans-cystic CBD exploration. This patient I did a cholangiogram on and there was a meniscus sign so I couldn't tell how big the stone was. I went trans-cystically and I found the stone. It was a single stone but the stone was almost a three centimeter in size and the The basket that I have is only about one, opens up to one point five centimeter in size.

The surgeon performs a choledochotomy to access the common bile duct. Using a choledochoscope, the surgeon deploys a basket behind a large stone and drags it upwards, attempting to remove it from the duct.

Another cheap way of doing it I guess is kind of doing a Fogarty Balloon. In this case I did not do a laser lithotripsy of the stone because it was just too big. It would have been spending a long time kind of removing those fragments.
The surgeon removes stone fragments from the common bile duct, following the breakage of a large stone during the procedure. The common bile duct is irrigated to clear remaining debris.

After irrigating the common bile duct, the surgeon uses a colodocuscope to inspect for stone fragments that have moved distally. The scope allows visualization both proximally and distally, aiding in the examination of the common hepatic duct and hepatic branches. The disposable ureteroscope is used for this inspection.

The surgeon uses a tool to grab and remove the stone fragments from the common bile duct. After stone removal, the common bile duct is closed with 5-0 PDS interrupted sutures. A completion cholangiogram is performed through the cystic duct opening to ensure the duct is clear.
